steps.content:
Let's talk about **Secrets**.

Everything you put in a pipeline (argument values, input defaults, run settings) is saved with it and visible to anyone who can access your Tangle instance. If they can open this pipeline or one of its runs, they can read those values.

So typing an API key or token straight into an argument quietly shares it with everyone, and writes it into the pipeline YAML too.

That's where **secrets** come in.

A secret keeps its value on the backend under a name you choose. Tangle only ever stores and sends the **name**. The value gets injected into the running container when the pipeline runs, and it never shows up anywhere in the app.

Let's create a secret and put it to use right in the **argument editor**.

Start by selecting the **Authenticated Request** task. Its inputs show up in the **Task Properties** panel on the right.

Now open the secret picker for the `token` argument.

Hover its row to reveal the **⚡** button, click it, then choose **Dynamic Data → Select Secret**.

Click **Add Secret** and give it a name and a value. The value is write only, so Tangle won't ever show it back to you. Save it, then pick it from the list to attach it to the argument.

Only the secret's **name** ends up in the pipeline.

Nice. The `token` argument now shows your secret **by name**, with a little lock icon, instead of its value.

The value stays on the backend and the pipeline only points at the name. Anyone you share this with sees the reference, never the credential.

Tangle has a dedicated **secrets manager**.

Open it by clicking the **Settings** gear in the top right.

This is **Settings → Secrets**, where all your secrets live. They're listed by name only, never by value.

You can **replace** a secret's value here (the change reaches every pipeline that uses it, and the value still never appears) or **delete** one you no longer need. Take a look, then carry on.

There's a second way to hand a secret to a pipeline, and it keeps the pipeline itself generic.

Instead of baking a specific secret into a task, you can leave a pipeline **input** empty and choose a secret for it **when you submit a run**. The pipeline definition then mentions no secret at all, and everyone can run it with their own.

This pipeline's **API_KEY** input was left blank for exactly that.
In **Runs and submission**, click the **split-arrows** icon next to Submit to open the run arguments dialog.

Find the **API_KEY** input, hover it, and click the **lock** ("Use Secret") button. Pick your secret to set it for this run. The value is supplied only at submit time and never saved into the pipeline.

Great! Now the pipeline will use the selected secret for the **API_KEY** input at runtime and the value will never be recorded in the pipeline definition.

Click **Submit Run** to launch the pipeline with your secret, then wrap up the tour.
